articles.title: Scientists discover why ozempic may not work for some people
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified genetic variants associated with reduced response to GLP-1 drugs used for type 2 diabetes. The report says about 10% of people may carry these variants and were less likely in clinical trials to reach healthy blood sugar targets while taking GLP-1 medications. The findings point to possible biological differences in treatment response rather than uniform effectiveness across patients.

articles.title: Popular GLP-1 weight-loss drugs linked to lower risks of addiction and overdose
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large study of more than 600,000 U.S. veterans found that GLP-1 drugs such as semaglutide were associated with lower risks of substance use disorders and related harms. Reported associations included fewer overdoses, hospitalizations, emergency visits, and drug-related deaths among people with addiction. Because this was an association study, it does not by itself prove the drugs directly prevent addiction outcomes.

articles.title: This new diabetes pill burns fat without the downsides of Ozempic
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers described an experimental pill for diabetes and obesity that works by increasing metabolism in skeletal muscle rather than suppressing appetite. According to the report, early clinical results suggest it lowered blood sugar and increased fat burning while preserving muscle mass. The article frames the drug as an alternative mechanism to current GLP-1-based therapies.

articles.title: Scientists discovered something surprising about french fries and diabetes
articles.summary_3_sentences: A long-term study of more than 205,000 people found that eating three servings of french fries per week was linked to a higher risk of type 2 diabetes. In contrast, baked, boiled, or mashed potatoes were not associated with a significant increase in risk in this report. The study also suggested replacing potatoes with whole grains was linked to lower diabetes risk.

articles.title: A study of 8,300 older adults revealed a surprising salt habit
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of older adults in Brazil found that adding salt at the table remained common, especially among men. The report links high salt intake to major health risks and notes that women's salt-use patterns were more closely tied to lifestyle and diet. The findings highlight a modifiable dietary behavior in an older population.

articles.title: The biggest collagen study yet reveals what actually works
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review involving nearly 8,000 participants found collagen supplements were associated with improved skin health and less osteoarthritis discomfort, particularly with longer and consistent use. Modest benefits were also reported for muscle and tendon health. The review found little evidence that collagen meaningfully improves sports recovery or post-exercise soreness.

articles.title: Intermittent fasting triggers surprising changes in the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: In adults with obesity, an intermittent fasting-style diet was associated with weight loss, improved metabolic markers, and changes in gut bacteria. Brain scans also showed changes in regions involved in appetite, cravings, and self-control. The study suggests a possible gut-brain component to dietary weight-loss responses, though it does not establish mechanism conclusively.

articles.title: This tomato-soy juice reduced inflammation in just four weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical study reported that adults with obesity who drank a tomato-soy juice daily for four weeks had reductions in several inflammatory blood markers. A comparison tomato juice did not show the same effect in the report. The findings suggest a short-term dietary intervention may influence inflammation-related measures in obesity.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut-brain circuit that triggers protein cravings
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a gut-brain signaling network that appears to drive animals to seek amino acids when protein is low. The report describes shifts in cravings away from sugar and toward protein-related nutrients under deficiency conditions. This is mechanistic research that could inform future work on appetite and nutrition.

articles.title: Vitamin B12 and folate deficiencies linked to chronic fatigue
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers in Japan reported that lower vitamin B12 and folate levels were linked to fatigue and reduced motivation, even in otherwise healthy people. The article presents these deficiencies as potentially overlooked contributors to persistent tiredness. The findings are observational and support further investigation rather than a definitive treatment claim.

articles.title: Omega-3 fish oil shows promise against type 2 diabetes
articles.summary_3_sentences: In diabetic rats, omega-3 fish oil supplementation improved blood sugar, cholesterol, and inflammation markers. The report says these effects were linked to shifts in immune cells toward a more anti-inflammatory state. Because the work was in rats, the findings are preclinical and not yet proof of benefit in humans.

articles.title: Beyond THC: Full Cannabis Extract Improves Glucose Levels
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that a full cannabis extract, rather than THC alone, improved measures of glucose regulation. It emphasizes that multiple cannabis compounds may contribute to metabolic effects. The piece presents the findings as early evidence of therapeutic potential for metabolic disorders.

articles.title: Creatine Supplements Benefit Muscles, Brain, and Overall Health
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says creatine supplementation may have benefits beyond athletic performance, including effects on muscle and brain health. Based on the limited information provided, it frames creatine as a broadly relevant nutritional supplement. Specific study details and populations are not clear from the source text.

articles.title: Beans and Soy May Lower Blood Pressure
articles.summary_3_sentences: This brief report states that higher intake of beans and soy may help lower blood pressure. It presents the foods as a dietary approach relevant to heart health. The provided information does not specify study design or population.

articles.title: Yo-yo Diet Fears May Be Unfounded
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says repeated cycles of weight loss and regain may be less harmful than previously believed. The piece challenges concerns around so-called yo-yo dieting based on a recent study. Further methodological details are not available in the provided text.

articles.title: Scientists reverse anxiety by fixing a tiny brain circuit
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a small group of amygdala neurons involved in anxiety and social behavior. Restoring normal activity in this circuit reversed anxiety-like and social deficits in mice. The work is preclinical and highlights a possible target for future therapies rather than an available treatment.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal two distinct types of autism
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study combining brain scans from nearly 1,000 people with autism and data from 20 mouse models identified two connectivity-based autism subtypes. One subtype showed hyperconnectivity and the other hypoconnectivity across brain regions. The findings support biological heterogeneity within autism and may guide more targeted future research.

articles.title: Scientists discover gut bacteria that may help protect against autism and ADHD
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported that epigenetic patterns present at birth may influence how the infant gut microbiome develops during the first year of life. Certain gene-microbe combinations were linked to early signs associated with autism and ADHD by age three. The study suggests an early developmental interaction between host biology and microbiome composition.

articles.title: Scientists found the hidden switch fueling alzheimer's brain inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified a molecular change affecting the protein STING that appears to keep brain immune activity elevated in Alzheimer's disease. The report links this process to neuroinflammation and damage to neuronal connections. The findings are mechanistic and may inform future therapeutic research.

articles.title: Protein traffic jams may explain aging, memory loss, and Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: In turquoise killifish, researchers found that the protein-making machinery becomes increasingly stalled with age. These ribosome collisions were linked to faulty proteins and protein clumps associated with neurodegeneration. The work is basic science that offers a possible mechanism connecting aging to brain decline.

articles.title: CBD may slow Alzheimer's by calming the brain's immune system
articles.summary_3_sentences: In experiments using Alzheimer's mice, inhaled CBD reduced markers of neuroinflammation. The report suggests CBD may affect immune pathways involved in brain degeneration. Because the evidence described is in mice, the findings are preclinical and not evidence of clinical benefit in people.

articles.title: Researchers block key protein that helps Parkinson's spread through the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified GPNMB as a protein that may help Parkinson's-related damage spread between cells in the brain. The report says antibodies blocking GPNMB interrupted this toxic process in early experiments. These findings are preliminary and point to a possible therapeutic target rather than a proven treatment.

articles.title: Scientists thought brain inflammation was driving long COVID but the scans told a different story
articles.summary_3_sentences: A brain imaging study found no evidence of widespread brain inflammation in people with long COVID. Instead, the most severe symptoms were associated with increased activity in regions related to mood and emotion. The results suggest a more specific neural pattern than a generalized inflammatory explanation.

articles.title: New depression treatment targets the immune system instead of the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical trial found that an anti-inflammatory drug used for rheumatoid arthritis appeared to improve symptoms in people with difficult-to-treat depression. Reported benefits also included reduced fatigue and anxiety and better quality of life. The findings are early and suggest immune-targeted treatment as a possible avenue for further study.

articles.title: Human organoids reveal how to reverse "irreversible" nerve damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used human brain-and-spinal-cord organoid systems to study why neurons lose regenerative capacity during development. They identified a gene network involved in this process and reported that an existing hormone drug boosted nerve fiber regrowth in the model. The work is preclinical and based on lab-grown systems rather than patients.

articles.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that sleep deprivation disrupted a brain circuit involved in social memory. In laboratory studies, caffeine restored signaling in that pathway and reversed the resulting memory deficit. The report describes a targeted effect in an experimental setting, not a broad clinical recommendation.

articles.title: Your brain starts making social decisions before you do
articles.summary_3_sentences: In zebrafish, researchers observed coordinated brain activity several seconds before the animals approached another fish. The pallium appeared important in this process, and stronger signals were associated with more social behavior. This is basic neuroscience research on how social decisions emerge in the brain.

articles.title: Eating Eggs Daily Linked to Lower Alzheimer's Risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that older adults who ate eggs daily or almost daily were less likely to develop Alzheimer's disease. It presents the finding as an association from observational research rather than proof that eggs prevent disease. Specific study methods are not detailed in the provided text.

articles.title: Increased Activity From Transposons ID'ed in Neurodegeneration
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report discusses increased activity of transposons, or "jumping genes," in neurodegeneration. It suggests that mobile genetic elements may contribute to disease-related genomic instability or pathology. The provided information is limited but points to a mechanistic genetics angle in neurodegenerative disorders.

articles.title: Understanding the Role of Tau in Neurodegenerative Disorders
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article focuses on tau, a protein strongly implicated in Alzheimer's disease and other neurodegenerative disorders. It appears to summarize research on tau's role in disease mechanisms and cognitive decline. The information provided is limited, but the item clearly concerns neurodegenerative biology.

articles.title: Scientists finally crack an "undruggable" pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial reported that daraxonrasib, a drug targeting KRAS-driven pancreatic cancer, nearly doubled survival in patients with advanced disease. The article says the treatment also reduced the risk of death by 60%. If confirmed, the findings would mark a significant advance against a cancer long considered difficult to target.

articles.title: Cancer's favorite escape trick may actually make it easier to kill
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that cancer cells that lose MHC I, a common strategy for avoiding killer T cells, may become more vulnerable to CD4+ helper T-cell responses. The work challenges a common assumption about immune evasion in cancer. It provides a mechanistic insight that could affect future immunotherapy strategies.

articles.title: A single protein may be holding back CAR T cancer therapy
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified NFIL3 as a protein that drives exhaustion in CAR T cells. Disabling NFIL3 helped the engineered cells remain active longer and control tumors better in animal models. The study is preclinical but suggests a way to improve durability of CAR T therapy.

articles.title: One fat helped pancreatic cancer grow while another cut disease in half
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice predisposed to pancreatic cancer, dietary oleic acid was associated with faster tumor growth, while omega-3-rich fish oil slowed disease development. The report suggests fat type may influence tumor biology more than total fat intake in this model. Because the findings come from mice, they should not be taken as direct dietary guidance for patients without further evidence.

articles.title: Why cancer spreads more in middle age than in old age
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mouse study found that melanoma spread was lowest in young mice, highest in middle-aged mice, and lower again in very old mice. The report implicates a type of immune cell that helps keep cancer dormant and limits metastasis. This is preclinical work on how age-related immune changes may affect cancer spread.

articles.title: The forgotten organ that could predict how long you live
articles.summary_3_sentences: Using AI analysis of CT scans from tens of thousands of adults, researchers found that healthier-looking thymuses were associated with lower risks of heart disease, cancer, and death. The study presents the thymus as a potentially informative marker of healthy aging and survival. The findings are observational and concern prediction rather than intervention.

articles.title: Scientists discover the master clock that controls biological growth and development
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers discovered a genetic timing system that coordinates bursts of gene activity during worm development. Disrupting this clock halted development in the model organism. The work is basic biology that may offer clues to developmental disorders and growth control.

articles.title: Scientists discover inherited traits that break Mendel's Laws of genetics
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mouse study reported inheritance patterns driven by epigenetic changes that do not follow classic Mendelian expectations. Researchers also described what they call the first naturally occurring paramutation identified in a mammal. The findings suggest non-DNA-sequence mechanisms may play a larger role in inheritance than previously appreciated.

articles.title: How Complex Genetic Interactions Can Lead to Varied Outcomes
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article describes how the same chromosome 16 deletion can lead to a broad range of neurodevelopmental outcomes. It emphasizes that interactions among genes may help explain why individuals with similar deletions can differ substantially. The piece highlights genetic complexity rather than a single deterministic effect.

articles.title: Revealing the Secrets of a Bacterial Enzyme in a Quest for New Antibiotics
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers studied the structure and function of a bacterial enzyme as a possible antibiotic target. The article says the findings provide insights that could support future drug development. This is early-stage basic and translational microbiology research.

articles.title: Scientists say evolution may work differently than we thought
articles.summary_3_sentences: A research study challenged the idea that most fixed genetic changes are neutral by arguing beneficial mutations are more common than often assumed. The report proposes that changing environments may prevent many advantageous mutations from sweeping through populations. This is evolutionary biology research with implications for how adaptation is understood.

articles.title: AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine passes first human trial
articles.summary_3_sentences: An AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine was reported to be safe and well tolerated in its first human trial. It generated immune responses against multiple coronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-2, SARS, and related bat viruses. The approach aims to target conserved viral features to broaden protection as coronaviruses evolve.

articles.title: A $4 tongue swab test detects tuberculosis within 30 minutes
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes a low-cost tongue swab test for active tuberculosis that can produce results in about 30 minutes. It is presented as a potentially useful option for people who have limited access to care or difficulty producing sputum samples. The test could improve practical TB detection if validated and deployed widely.

articles.title: Medical Cannabis Linked to Lower Opioid Use for Pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that medical cannabis use was linked to lower opioid use among people with chronic pain. It frames cannabis as a potential adjunct that may help reduce reliance on opioid medications. The provided information supports an association, not proof that cannabis directly causes opioid reduction.

articles.title: Cannabis capsules ease severe chronic arthritis pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says cannabis capsules reduced severe chronic arthritis pain in a recent study. It presents cannabis as a pain-management option for joint disease symptoms. Details on trial size and design are not included in the provided text.

articles.title: Hidden driving danger when edible cannabis and alcohol mix
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research from Johns Hopkins found that combining edible cannabis with alcohol produced greater driving impairment than either substance alone. The report also says common field sobriety tests often failed to detect the cannabis-related impairment. The findings have implications for traffic safety and impairment assessment.

articles.title: Travel May Slow Aging and Improve Health
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says travel may have beneficial effects on health and may slow signs of aging. The provided text is limited and does not specify whether the piece is based on observational or interventional evidence. It is best read as a lifestyle-oriented health report rather than definitive proof of anti-aging benefit.

articles.title: Repairing DNA damage: Scientists discover a surprising new benefit of melatonin
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study suggested melatonin supplements may enhance DNA repair in night shift workers. The article frames this as a possible way to reduce some biological damage associated with overnight work. The findings are early and do not yet establish long-term clinical benefit.

articles.title: A silent kidney crisis is spreading far faster than experts expected
articles.summary_3_sentences: A global study reported that chronic kidney disease affects nearly 800 million people and is a leading cause of death worldwide. The article emphasizes that the condition is often silent early on and contributes substantially to cardiovascular disease. It underscores chronic kidney disease as a major public health burden.

articles.title: A hidden pollutant is changing how the world's forests breathe
articles.summary_3_sentences: A global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can either accelerate or slow forest soil respiration depending on ecosystem conditions. The report highlights tipping points that may affect carbon storage and forest responses to climate change. This is environmental science with implications for ecosystem health.

articles.title: The ocean's health may depend on a tiny microbe inside fish
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found evidence that gut microbes in marine fish help produce calcium carbonate, a mineral important to ocean chemistry and carbon storage. The work suggests fish and microbes act together in a process previously attributed mainly to fish alone. The findings add a microbial dimension to understanding ocean health.

articles.title: Beluga whales keep switching mates and it may be saving their species
articles.summary_3_sentences: DNA analysis of more than 600 beluga whales over 13 years showed that both males and females often had offspring with multiple partners over their lifetimes. The study describes a flexible mating system in Alaska's Bristol Bay population. Such flexibility may help maintain genetic diversity in the species.

articles.title: The secret to pigeons' incredible navigation was hiding in their liver
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported that iron-rich immune cells in the pigeon liver may function as magnetic sensors. Birds lacking these cells had more difficulty navigating under overcast skies. The study offers a possible explanation for magnetically guided navigation in birds.

articles.title: This newly discovered raptor may have hunted like a giant heron
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists described a newly identified dinosaur from Patagonia, Kank australis, with features suggesting it hunted fish in a heron-like manner. The report highlights its long flexible neck and specialized vertebrae. The fossil adds to understanding of ecological diversity among ancient predators.

articles.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles' lost crocodiles
articles.summary_3_sentences: DNA from museum specimens showed that the Seychelles' vanished crocodiles were not a distinct species. Instead, they were an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely dispersed across the Indian Ocean. The finding resolves a long-standing zoological mystery.

articles.title: A 100-year-old piano mystery has finally been solved
articles.summary_3_sentences: Using high-speed sensing of piano key motion, researchers found that a performer's touch can alter perceived tone color. The study suggests elite pianists make subtle key movements that listeners can hear. This is a science-of-music finding rather than a medical item.

articles.title: Forget LASIK: Safer, cheaper vision correction without lasers or surgery
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers are developing a non-surgical vision-correction approach that uses mild electrical pulses and platinum contact lenses to soften and reshape the cornea. Early tests on rabbit eyes corrected nearsightedness in about a minute while preserving eye structure. The work is preclinical and has not yet demonstrated safety or efficacy in humans.
